title: Surjective does not imply injective on an infinite domain
informal_statement: It is not the case that every surjective function f : ℕ -> ℕ is injective. The finite pigeonhole fact (surjective implies injective between equal-size finite sets) does not generalize to the infinite domain ℕ: witnessed by f(n) = n - 1 (Nat truncated subtraction), which is surjective (f(m+1) = m for every m) but not injective (f(0) = f(1) = 0).
informal_proof_idea: Assume the universal claim for contradiction, instantiate at f = (n |-> n - 1), discharge surjectivity via the witness n = m + 1, then derive 0 = 1 from injectivity applied to the equal images f(0) = f(1) = 0, closing by decide.
formal_statement_pp: theorem surjective_not_always_injective : ¬ (∀ f : ℕ → ℕ, Function.Surjective f → Function.Injective f)

review.review_basis: repo self-review; kernel-verified through the tracked proof-search loop

notes: Proof authority: Lean kernel via the tracked proof-search loop (episode 0b3fa2fb, problem_version d8740856-06cf-4265-9564-11aff3106297). Companion to the negative example negative.functions.surjective_implies_injective.finite_pigeonhole_misapplied.v1, which captures the natural-but-false direct attempt on the unconditional claim (simp [Function.Injective], leaving an unsolved_goals residual) in a separate tracked episode (a1bfdf14).
